title: Vintage Gas Pump Cabinet with Side Bookshelves
summary: A tall storage cabinet styled as an old gas pump, flanked by two matching bookshelves — a single door and a round "gauge" cutout sell the look.
description:
This is a real storage cabinet — a face-framed bookshelf box with a single hinged door — dressed up as a vintage gas pump, flanked by two smaller bookshelf towers built as separate, identical units. Building the side bookshelves as their own boxes rather than one continuous wide shelf is what lets the center cabinet stand slightly proud of them and read as the "pump" against two supporting towers.

The theming details are what make this project: a round disc cut from 1x12 becomes the pump's gauge face, a length of black flex tubing becomes the hose, and a scrap-cut handle finishes the illusion. None of that is structural — build the cabinet and shelves solidly first, then treat the gas-pump details as trim you add at the end, so a mistake on the fun stuff never costs you the carcass.

tools.note: Cuts the round gauge-face disc and the decorative handle.

cutList.note: Two per side bookshelf, two side bookshelves total.
Cut into an 11" circle with a jigsaw.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: From the 1x12s cut two cabinet sides 48", five shelves 15", the 43" door and an 11" blank for the gauge disc. The three 1x12 boards break down cleanly: one 8-footer gives a 48" side plus three 15" shelves, the other gives the second 48" side plus the 43" door, and the 4 ft board gives the last two shelves and the 11" disc blank. Cut the 1/4" plywood back 16-1/2" x 48"; from the 10 ft 1x3 cut the 16-1/2" bottom rail and two 43-1/4" stiles; from 1x4 cut the 16-1/2" top rail, four bookshelf verticals 44" and six bookshelf shelves 9-3/4". From 1x2 cut ten shelf trims 9-3/4", two short verticals 11-1/4", two short horizontals 4-1/4" and the 18" top strip. Both 1x2s and all three 1x4s are consumed almost exactly, so cut the longest pieces first and keep the offcuts — the pump handle has no cut-list row and has to come out of 1x4 scrap.

steps.title: Build the main cabinet box
steps.body: The two 48" sides run the full height and the five 15" shelves fit between them — that is what makes the 16-1/2" x 48" plywood back cover the carcass exactly, since 15" plus 3/4" of side stock at each edge is 16-1/2". Set the top and bottom shelves flush with the ends of the sides; the plan does not fix the three middle shelf heights, so space them for what you intend to store. Join every shelf with pocket holes and 1-1/4" screws or with glue and brads — both hold here, because the back and the face frame lock the box in the next steps. Square the carcass on the diagonals, then glue and nail the back on, hitting every shelf edge as you go; that back is what keeps it square permanently.

steps.title: Build and attach the face frame
steps.body: Assemble the face frame flat on the bench with the 16-1/2" rails running the full width and the 43-1/4" stiles fitted between them, so the frame's width matches the carcass exactly. Check the arithmetic before you glue: 2-1/2" plus 43-1/4" plus 3-1/2" is 49-1/4" against a 48" cabinet side, so something has to give — the plan does not say what, so dry-fit against your actual box and trim the stiles to suit. Clamp it square, then glue it to the front of the carcass with 1-1/4" brads, outside edges flush. Measure the opening the finished frame leaves before you cut the door to final size.

steps.title: Build both side bookshelves
steps.body: Each tower is two 44" 1x4 verticals with three 9-3/4" 1x4 shelves fitted between them, which makes the tower 11-1/4" across the front — the same as the cabinet's 11-1/4" depth, so all three units line up front to back. Build both towers identically and flat on the bench, glue and brads at each shelf. The five 9-3/4" 1x2 trims per tower run across the front shelf edges and stiffen the shallow shelves as much as they dress them. Pocket holes are worth the trouble on the top and bottom joints, where a tall narrow tower takes the most racking.

steps.title: Attach the side bookshelves and trim
steps.body: Attach the side bookshelves to the main cabinet, working from the back forward, cutting trim pieces to fit as you go rather than pre-cutting everything. Nail on with 1-1/4" nails and glue.

steps.title: Add the gauge, base feet, and door
steps.body: Draw an 11" circle on the 11" blank, cut it with the jigsaw staying just outside the line, then sand back to the line — this is the one detail that reads 'gas pump' from across the room, and a lumpy circle shows from there too. Mount it centered on the upper cabinet face. Screw the two 5-1/2" square base feet under the cabinet, set back from the front edge so they do not show. The door is a single 43" length of 1x12: hang it on its hinges first, check the reveal all the way round, and only then fit the magnetic clasp.

steps.title: Add the hose, handle, and finish details
steps.body: Mount the 2-hole strap and run the black flex tubing from it to suggest the pump's hose. Cut a handle shape from 1x4 scrap, paint it, and attach it to the door. Fill nail holes with wood filler, sand, and finish or paint as desired — a hand-lettered or stenciled sign on top completes the look.

images.alt: Storage cabinet styled as a vintage gas pump with matching side bookshelves
